title: Military Engagements in Spanish and Mexican California
subtitle: Battles, raids, revolts, and standoffs, 1769–1848 — from the military register
abstract: Every engagement plotted here is documented in the project's military register, compiled from the C-A transcripts (Archives of California), Bancroft, Cook, and printed primary collections. Hollow or dashed markers mean the location is approximate or conjectural — the source names a district, not a spot. Register numbers (#) key each pin to the register's fuller entry and sources.

features.name: Battle at San Diego Mission
features.summary: First armed clash in Alta California. Spanish exploring party attacked at Cosoy. 1 Spaniard killed (blacksmith José Manuel Robles), 3 natives killed.

features.name: Destruction of San Diego Mission
features.summary: 800–1,000 attackers from 9+ rancherías; dual-axis plan (mission + presidio). Defenders: Cpl. Rocha + 3 soldiers, 2 friars, 3 artisans = 11 men. Padre Jayme martyred (18 arrow wounds). Blacksmith Arroyo killed. Carpenter Urselino died of wounds. Cpl. Rocha fired muskets all night from adobe enclosure.
features.result: INDIGENOUS TACTICAL VICTORY — first successful mission destruction

quote.es: "Amad á Dios, hijos" — Jayme's last words. Urselino left his pay "for the benefit of his murderers."

sources.citation: Fuster, Registro de Defunciones (eyewitness). Ortega, Informe Nov. 30, 1775. Bancroft I, 250-254. C-A 1 (Prov. St. Pap. i.228-32). C-A 15, n5 (ranchería list).

features.name: Carrillo's Punishment at Alocuachomi
features.summary: Punitive raid near San Juan Capistrano. 3 natives killed.

features.name: Ambush at San Juan (Briones killed)
features.summary: Midnight ambush near San Diego. Corporal Briones killed.

features.name: Carrillo's Raid on Pamó
features.summary: Dawn assault on Pamó ranchería. 4 chiefs executed — first public execution in California.

features.name: Tenoqui Attack on Prospecting Soldiers
features.summary: Santa Bárbara backcountry. 2 soldiers killed, Spanish driven off.

features.name: San Diego Uprising: Ranchería Participant List
features.summary: Full intelligence from C-A 15: 13+ rancherías identified. Gentile woman warned of second attack planned.

features.name: Battle at La Ciénega
features.summary: From hoja de servicios of Sargento Verdugo: under Capt. Rivera, "estuvieron el ataque que hicieron los gentiles en el parage de la Ciénega, en que se mataron 3."

features.name: Toypurina's Revolt at San Gabriel
features.summary: MAJOR CONSPIRACY. Toypurina — female war leader of the Tongva (Gabrieleño) — organized multi-village uprising against Mission San Gabriel with neophyte alcalde Nicolás José. Plan to kill padres and soldiers. Betrayed by informant before attack could succeed. Toypurina and co-conspirators arrested. She was exiled to Mission San Carlos Borromeo. One of the most important indigenous military actors in California history.
features.result: Conspiracy foiled — Toypurina arrested and exiled

sources.citation: Beebe & Senkewicz, pp. 247–249. Bancroft I. Hugo Reid letters.

features.name: Yuma Revolt (Colorado River Massacre)
features.summary: DECISIVE YUMA VICTORY. 2 mission-pueblos (La Purísima Concepción, San Pedro y San Pablo) destroyed. 46+ dead including 4 Franciscan friars. Overland route from Sonora to California closed for 40+ years.
features.result: DECISIVE YUMA VICTORY — overland route closed

sources.citation: Arricivita, Crónica Seráfica (1792). Bancroft I. C-A 22.

features.name: Death of Rivera y Moncada
features.summary: Sub-engagement of Yuma Revolt. Capt. Fernando de Rivera y Moncada and all 11–12 soldiers killed at separate camp on east bank. Rivera was the military governor of the Californias.

sources.citation: C-A 22, n300, pp. 286–88. Neve to Com. Gral.

features.name: Fages at Ruins of Purísima
features.summary: Fages arrives at "arruinado pueblo de la Purísima." Yumas have fled. Communicates with Romeu and Fueros.

quote.es: "Los Yumas se han retirado y los demás gentiles son pacíficos."

features.name: Yuma Pacification Strategy
features.summary: Fages proposes withdrawing troops to let Yumas return to agriculture, then apprehending ringleaders.

quote.es: "Sin cuyo logro se dificultaría la aprehensión de los cabecillas."

features.name: Rivera's Final Expedition (written before massacre)
features.summary: Neve writes from San Gabriel: 17 families departed Loreto under Alférez Ramón Laso. Sargento Juan José Robles + 12 soldiers heading to Colorado to meet Rivera — who would be killed 2 months later.

features.name: Colorado River Attacks (3 incidents)
features.summary: Gentiles attacked 3 times, killed 5 people on the Colorado River frontier. Eastern boundary violence.

features.name: Colorado River: 11 of 21 Killed
features.summary: "De los 21 que eran se mataron allí once, y que 10 eran los fugados." Fr. Señan reports 10 killed. Solá orders vigía on Colorado River Indians.

features.name: Fages — El Canelo Skirmish
features.summary: First engagement of Fages' punitive expedition after Yuma Revolt. ~1 hour of fighting at El Canelo. 3 volunteers and 2 presidials wounded. Rescued 2 captive women and a child from Yuma captivity.

sources.citation: Fages, Colorado River Campaign diary (Priestley, 1913). Vault: sources/fages-colorado-river-campaign-1781.md

features.name: Fages — Battle of October 20
features.summary: Major engagement of Fages' Colorado River campaign. Killed 25+ Yumas including Palma's son and brother (badly wounded). Previously ransomed 48 captives (Oct 18), 14 more (Oct 19). Officers voted unanimously to retreat Oct 21 despite Fages' intent to continue.
features.result: Spanish tactical victory — 25+ Yuma killed, 62 captives ransomed

sources.citation: Fages, Colorado River Campaign diary (Priestley, 1913). Vault: sources/fages-colorado-river-campaign-1781.md

features.name: Sacalanes Attack on Neophyte Party
features.summary: East Bay hills, Sacalan territory (Alameda Co. between Oakland and Mission SJ). Dantí sent ~30 neophytes (no soldiers) across the bay to recover fugitives. Sacalanes attacked, killed 8–10. Gov. Borica refused retaliation.

features.name: Briones vs. Tulares Ranchería
features.summary: Tulare Valley edge, near SLO. Punitive raid; chief captured.

features.name: Amador vs. Sacalanes/Cuchillones
features.summary: Sacalanes ranchería, ~6 leagues from Mission SJ, in redwoods (palos colorados) and sierras — probably Sunol/Niles Canyon area. Sgt. Pedro Amador with 22 soldiers vs. ~50 Sacalanes/Cuchillones. Sacalanes dug pit traps (pozos) to neutralize cavalry — forced Spanish to dismount and fight hand-to-hand for 2+ hours. 7–8 killed.
features.result: Spanish victory — but innovative indigenous counter-cavalry tactics

features.name: Castro's Night Siege at SJB
features.summary: Night siege of Mission San Juan Bautista. 1 chief killed, 1 soldier wounded.

features.name: Amador's Raid from Santa Clara
features.summary: Hills east of Santa Clara. Chief killed, weapons broken, captives taken.

sources.citation: Bancroft II. PP 0254 (Alberni orders 12 armed men for Amador).

features.name: Pacheco's Fight near Santa Clara
features.summary: 5 gentiles killed near Santa Clara.

features.name: Capture of Chief Guchapa
features.summary: Cholam village. Spanish captured chief; negotiated release.

features.name: Attack on Padre Cueva
features.summary: Ranchería 10–15 miles east of Mission San José, in Asisines/Luecheas territory (Diablo Range foothills). Spanish driven back.

features.name: Fight at Carquinez Strait
features.summary: Carquinez Strait narrows. 12–15 Mission SJ neophytes killed by gentiles controlling the strait crossing.

features.name: Ranchería Attack Near San José
features.summary: 6 leagues from San José. Multi-day pursuit. 15 volunteers + 18 soldiers. 11 killed, 31 captured.

features.name: San Juan Bautista Wheat-Field Arson
features.summary: INDIGENOUS ECONOMIC WARFARE. Wheat crop burned. Moraga captured capitanejo from Santa Cruz (pursued 3 years). Signs of sublevación.

features.name: Amador's Failed Attack at San José
features.summary: "Salió nulo el proyecto de atacar los Gentiles." Failed military operation by Sargento Pedro Amador.

features.name: Fugitive Neophytes: Sacalan & Cuchillones
features.summary: 3 Christians fled Mission SF to their lands: 1 Sacalan, 2 Cuchillones. Tribal identification of fugitives.

sources.citation: C-A 9, n30, p. 49. Fray Martín Landaeta.

features.name: Amador Expedition with Captives
features.summary: Amador reports on expedition from SF jurisdiction. Parte y diario original. Awaits orders about "la presa que recogió" (captives).

features.name: Indian Killed at Monterey Presidio
features.summary: Indian Choychi grabbed musket at 2 AM, wounded guard Quesadas with bayonet. Soldiers entered; Marín struck him on head — died 1½ hours later "echando sangre con espuma por la boca."

features.name: Murder of Christian Indian Toribio
features.summary: Padre sent Christians to fetch gentile woman. Old gentile man + 2 sons killed Toribio — suspected him of poisoning ("enyerbado") their daughter who died at mission. Marcos Briones dispatches soldiers.

features.name: Expedition against Tulares Gathering
features.summary: Pursued fugitives, couldn't find them. Indian woman reported gentiles gathering "heading toward the Tulares." Plans to return "to pacify the gathering of Gentiles."

features.name: Amador Expedition to el Colorado
features.summary: Argüello orders Amador: "A lo gentiles no se los violente para hacerlos de sus rancherías." Gentiles fled "porque decían que querían bautizarlos" — feared forced baptism.

quote.es: "A lo gentiles no se los violente."

features.name: False Alarm: Attack on SLO, SM, Concepción
features.summary: Rumored multi-mission attack proved false. Viceroy Azanza "queda impuesto de la falsedad de la noticia."

features.name: Tulares: Borica Orders Avoidance
features.summary: "No conviene de ningún modo mandar Neofitos ni tropa á los Tulares... por ser muchísimos los Gentiles y exponerse á una pelea fuerte." Sargento Macario Castro sent with Indian guides instead.

features.name: Peralta's Failed Expedition from Santa Clara
features.summary: Cabo Luis Peralta (future grantee of Rancho San Antonio — Oakland/Berkeley/Alameda) "did not have satisfactory results for lack of good guides." Can't navigate into indigenous territory.

features.name: Report at Pizcache Village
features.summary: No combat. Oral memory of prior battle: "Other soldiers from the other side of the mountains appeared about twenty years ago." ~200 souls, chief Sujoyucomu.

features.name: Armed Confrontation at Río Guadalupe
features.summary: Tuolumne River area. 40 armed Indians with feather flag. Standoff; no casualties.

features.name: Encounter at Taulamne
features.summary: Tuolumne caves. ~200 souls on steep cliffs, inaccessible. Indians live in caves. Refused to descend. Said "soldiers killed and captured people." Armed passive resistance.

features.name: Skirmish at Apaglamenes/Tationes
features.summary: Near Grayson, Stanislaus County. 30 armed heathen on opposite bank, "painted, bedecked with feathers." 12 shots fired. One Indian hit. "Soldier Morales came into the skirmish with half his chin shaved."

features.name: Dawn Assault at Pescadero/Cholvones
features.summary: Delta — Old River area. "Before dawn we assaulted a village." 15 San José Christians recovered, 18 heathen men captured, 51 women (released — "went away very happy"). 2 prisoners escaped.

features.name: Battle of Suisun
features.summary: MAJOR ENGAGEMENT. Alf. Moraga, 17 cavalry vs. ~120 Sespesuña/Suisun warriors. Crossed Carquines Strait by boat. Hard fight — 18 captured (released, expected to die). Survivors in 3 huts: 2 stormed, all killed; 3rd set on fire — "bravely preferred to perish in the flames." 4 Spanish wounded (23.5% rate) — highest in any Spanish victory. Royal Order from Spain praised "glorious action." Moraga promoted; Herrera + Soto made sergeants.
features.result: Spanish tactical victory — highest casualty rate of any Spanish victory

quote.es: Chief Malaca: "Es mejor morir libres que vivir como esclavos, despreciados por nuestro Puy."

sources.citation: Prov. Rec. IX.122-3 (C-A 25). Prov. St. Pap. XIX.314 (C-A 12). Bancroft II, 91-92. Vallejo, Recuerdos, i.131-5.

features.name: Tense Encounter with Tauquimenes
features.summary: Sacramento River branch, delta. Standoff; no combat.

features.name: Soto's Battle
features.summary: LARGEST PITCHED BATTLE IN COLONIAL CA. Sgt. Soto + 12 soldiers from SF (by boat) + 100 Mission San José auxiliaries vs. ~1,000 warriors from 4 united rancherías (Unsumnes). Warriors pre-positioned on marshy island, women/children removed. "Fell upon the soldiers at landing." 3-hour battle. Soto "was at one time doubtful of the result, so reliant were the foes on their numbers, so careless of life." 1 neophyte killed (Alcalde Julio); many indigenous dead. Not a single fugitive captured.
features.result: Spanish tactical / INDIGENOUS STRATEGIC VICTORY — largest single-engagement indigenous army in CA

sources.citation: Soto, Expedicion Militar del Sargento, 1813, MS. (in Argüello report). S. José Lib. Mision, 25. Bancroft II, 324-5. C-A 12 (Prov. St. Pap. XIX.334-348). Cook, Colonial Expeditions, 265-277.

features.name: Pico's Fight at San Joaquin Crossing
features.summary: San Joaquin River between Dos Palos and Los Banos. Day 9: armed heathen crossing river, Cpl. Juarez engaged — killed 2. Day 10: Indians came to attack camp at dawn. Line of battle formed. Interpreter asked what they wanted — "to fight." After firing, troops dismounted and pursued through brush. 3 killed, 1 captured. Dead included a Christian from Santa Cruz who was "the one most determined" to kill the soldiers.

features.name: Ortega's Raid on Tache
features.summary: Tache village, Kings River, not far west of Lemoore (Cook note [2]). Ortega attacked at dawn but village deserted — fugitive Indians had warned them "the soldiers were coming to kill them at the point of the lance." Found 3 Soledad horses. Followed into tule swamp but could not reach inhabitants.

sources.citation: Ortega diary (Cook, pp. 267–269). Cook note [2]: Tachi territory west of Tulare Lake, villages near Lemoore.

features.name: Pico's Raid on Cheneche
features.summary: Cheneche village near mouth of Mariposa Creek, north of Los Banos, on the San Joaquin River. Pre-dawn assault at 4 AM. 66 captured (Christians and heathen). Released 4 men and 12 crippled old women. "Majority of the people escaped because this village is in a bad location." Found 7 live horses and 5 recently dead.
features.result: Mostly escaped — bad terrain for pursuit

sources.citation: Pico diary (Cook, pp. 270–271). Cook note [9]: Cheneches near mouth of Mariposa Creek.

features.name: Tapee Horse Slaughter
features.summary: Village of Tapee (Tape), south of the great bend of the San Joaquin River, ~20 miles west of Fresno (Cook note [9]). Pico surrounded village on both sides of river. Found 238 RECENTLY KILLED ANIMALS, "a great deal of meat quartered and dried," and 16 live animals shot with arrows or badly treated. "From Tapee to Cheneches... more than 500 dead animals." LARGEST INDIGENOUS ECONOMIC WARFARE INCIDENT.
features.result: INDIGENOUS ECONOMIC WARFARE — 238+ horses killed, 500+ in region

sources.citation: Pico diary (Cook, pp. 275–276). Cook note [9]: Tape ~20 mi. W of Fresno, south of SJ great bend.

features.name: Search for Malim Village
features.summary: Junction of Mariposa Creek and the San Joaquin River, near Los Banos. Guide (from Malim village) told them he knew the way, then when they arrived said he didn't know where it was — "but he did know that this was his country and we were hunting for his village." Guide given 10 lashes. Village found but inhabitants had escaped. 2 Christians from Santa Cruz captured.

sources.citation: Pico diary (Cook, pp. 277–278). Cook note [9]: Malim between Cheneches and Nupchenches along SJ River.

features.name: Martinez Incident at Bubal
features.summary: Village of Bubal (Wowol), western shore of Tulare Lake (Cook note [6]). Fr. Luis Antonio Martinez burned village of Pusas. Lively controversy with other friars over his behavior.

sources.citation: Cook, pp. 279–286. Cook note [6]: Bubal = Wowol, NE shore of Tulare Lake.

features.name: Battle at Malime/Tasnil
features.summary: Near Santa Cruz. Inter-indigenous: Christians vs. gentiles. 4 killed.

features.name: Armed Encounter on Sacramento
features.summary: Sacramento River, ~50 miles from SF. Standoff; Spanish retreated.

features.name: Skirmish with Fugitives on Sacramento
features.summary: Sacramento delta island. Minor skirmish; 1 wounded.

features.name: Campaign against Muquelenis
features.summary: Mokelumne, eastern delta. Spanish reported success.

features.name: Garcia's Battle at Jayaya/Tape
features.summary: Beyond Santa Rita, near SJB. Spanish nearly defeated; chaotic retreat.

features.name: Boronda's Campaign at Tachi
features.summary: Tachi village, west of Tulare Lake near Lemoore (Cook note [2]: "not far west of Lemoore"). Same village Ortega raided in Nov 1815. "Arrows raining; underground defenses." Indigenous fortification.

sources.citation: Bancroft II. Cook note [2] on Tachi location.

features.name: Moraga's Failed Expedition to Amajava
features.summary: Interior Santa Barbara. "Impassable roads, no water/pasture. Sin haber podido llegar á la ranchería de los Amajavas." Gentiles attacked others as reprisal for mission punishment.

features.name: Killing of Padre Quintana at Santa Cruz
features.summary: Neophytes at Mission Santa Cruz killed Padre Andrés Quintana after he used a wire-tipped whip on them. Lorenzo Asisara's eyewitness account (recorded 1877) is the primary source. Planned conspiracy among neophytes — lured padre out at night, strangled and mutilated him. Death concealed as natural for years.
features.result: Padre killed — indigenous resistance to mission abuse

sources.citation: Lorenzo Asisara account (Beebe & Senkewicz, pp. 284–293). Bancroft II.

features.name: Sánchez SF Campaign
features.summary: SF area. Solá: campaign under Sargento Sánchez "fue muy brillante." Recommended for alférez despite near-invalid status.

features.name: Battle of Punta de Guijarros (Lelia Byrd)
features.summary: FIRST NAVAL ENGAGEMENT in California. Shore battery vs. American frigate. Grapeshot exchanged; ship escaped.

features.name: Battle at Santa Catalina Frontera
features.summary: Baja California frontier. Spanish victory in two phases; chief killed. Sargento Salgado wounded — musket ball lodged.

features.name: English Ship at Bodega with Cannons on Land
features.summary: English ship anchored at Bodega with cannons on land — possible temporary fortification. Same year as Spanish Bodega colonization failure.

features.name: De la Cuadra Emergency Battery at Monterey
features.summary: Naval captain from Nootka finds NO defense at Monterey landing. Orders 4-cannon battery built on overlooking hill.

features.name: English Launches at Santa Cruz
features.summary: English launches at Santa Cruz coast; withdrew morning of Nov 28. Sargento Pedro Amador sent as reinforcement. Soldier Joaquín Mesa punished for misreporting.

features.name: San Diego Harbor Defense Protocol
features.summary: Detailed defensive orders: artillery alternates 24-hr guard; live sentinel at batería; watch bocana del Estrecho. English aware of SJC fondeadero. Pedreros recommended at SJC embarcadero.

features.name: American Vessel Captured at Refugio
features.summary: Sargento Carlos Carrillo reported 2 vessels. Alférez with 14 men captured 2 Americans + 2 Indians + 1 negro. Then 6 more Americans + 2 captains. Goleta prisionera.

features.name: Post-Bouchard Defense: 150 Machetes
features.summary: Solá + Fr. Payeras: construct 150 machetes + cartucheras — "por el corto número de espadas que tienen." Model machetes sent to missions for production. Desperate arming.

features.name: English Corsair Campaign — CA Response
features.summary: 4 English frigates (22–24 guns each) raided Gulf of California shipping. Gov. Arrillaga: "sin ningunos elementos de defensa." 50-man militia raised; battery formed at Puerto Escondido.
features.result: English naval dominance — Spanish desperate defense

features.name: Bouchard's Attack on Monterey
features.summary: MAJOR NAVAL RAID. Argentine privateer Bouchard: Argentina (38 guns + 2 howitzers) + Santa Rosa (26 guns), ~285 crew. Spanish: 40 defenders (25 cavalry, 4 veteran artillery, 11 militia). Nov 21: 2-hour artillery duel — J.J. Vallejo's beach battery did most damage; 5+ insurgents killed. Nov 22: 400 men landed with 4 field pieces; Estrada's 25 men overwhelmed. Solá retreated to Salinas, saving archives + 6,000 cartridges. Presidio burned Nov 26-27. Damage: ~$10,000. Only foreign capture of a California capital.
features.result: Only time a California presidio fell to an enemy — Monterey burned

quote.en: Guerra's trick at Sta Barbara: marched troops round a hill repeatedly to look like a large army.

sources.citation: Solá, Noticia (report to viceroy). Prov. Rec. XII.164 (C-A 26). Prov. St. Pap. XX.228-30 (C-A 13). Ben. Mil. XLIX (C-A 17). 15+ Californio testimonies. Bancroft II, 227-243.

features.name: Bouchard's Raid on Refugio Ranch
features.summary: Dec 2: Bouchard landed, plundered and burned Ortega's ranch, killed cattle. Sgt. Carlos Carrillo with 30 men reached Sta Inés at 11:30 PM. Padres sent 40 armed neophytes + 35 more from SLO. Spanish ambush party captured 3 insurgents (Lt. William Taylor of Boston, Martin Romero, Mateo José Pascual).
features.result: Ranch destroyed; 3 insurgents captured by ambush

sources.citation: Padre Ullibarri to Payeras (Arch. Arzob. III.ii.24-6). Bancroft II, 236-237.

features.name: Bouchard at San Juan Capistrano
features.summary: Dec 14-15: Alf. Santiago Argüello with 30 men from San Diego posted on hill. Bouchard landed, requested supplies, refused. Minor damage — straw houses burned, wine/brandy spilled. 4 insurgent deserters surrendered (John Rose, Scotch drummer; Pedro Zaldíbar; Nicolás Chavarría; Mateo José Pascual).

sources.citation: Bancroft II, 240-241. Prov. St. Pap. XX.62; Ben. Mil. XLIX.66-72 (prisoner testimony).

features.name: Santa Clara Uprising Threat
features.summary: Fray Diego de Noboa warns: rancherías augmented, "rompimiento muy semejante al de Sonora" (uprising like Sonora). Exiled gentiles from Monterey/Santa Cruz fled with Christians. "Situación muy crítica."

features.name: Stockade at Mission San José
features.summary: Estacada ordered: 70 varas long, 60 varas wide. Soldiers must carry arms at all times; weekly arms and cartridge inspection. Pedro Amador instructions.

features.name: Indian Prisoners at San Diego
features.summary: "Indios presidiarios" maintained at $693/year at San Diego presidio. Indigenous prisoners as fiscal/military burden.

features.name: Neophyte Hermenegildo Condemned to Death
features.summary: Neophyte sentenced to capital punishment at San Diego. Extreme measure.

features.name: Arms Inventory ALL CA Presidios
features.summary: Complete "Relación del Armamento" at San Gabriel. ALL 5 presidios: 263 swords (72 short), 376 mohanas (22 short), 289 escopetas (51 short), 434 pistol pairs. SF: 32 swords (13 short), 55 mohanas, 39 escopetas (12 short).

features.name: Chumash Revolt — Santa Inés
features.summary: LARGEST INDIGENOUS UPRISING IN CALIFORNIA. Trigger: flogging of Purísima neophyte by Cpl. Cota. Feb 21 afternoon: neophytes attacked soldiers, set fire to "a large part of mission buildings." Sgt. Anastasio Carrillo arrived next day with reinforcements. Hostile Indians fled to Purísima.
features.result: Chumash driven off — revolt spreads to La Purísima and Santa Barbara

sources.citation: Rafael González (Beebe & Senkewicz, pp. 323-365). Bancroft II, 527-536. Ripoll, Levantamiento, MS.

features.name: Chumash Revolt — La Purísima Seized
features.summary: Same afternoon as Sta Inés. Cpl. Tapia + 4-5 soldiers defended families through night; surrendered when powder ran out. 4 white travelers killed (Sepúlveda, Sotelo + 2). 7 Indians killed. ~400 neophytes then fortified mission: palisades, loopholes in adobe, mounted old cannon + 2 swivel-guns, drilled for month. Padre Rodríguez stayed — "no disposition to molest him."
features.result: INDIGENOUS VICTORY — Chumash held fortified mission for 29 days

quote.en: Only time indigenous fighters used artillery in California.

sources.citation: Bancroft II, 528-533. Purísima Lib. Mis., MS., 11. Beebe & Senkewicz, 323-365.

features.name: Chumash Revolt — Santa Barbara
features.summary: Feb 22: Neophytes under Andrés armed themselves. Fought Capt. Guerra's troops "several hours" from behind corridor pillars with guns and arrows. Indians: 2 killed, 3 wounded (28 total dead later). Spanish: 4 wounded. Indians took property (respected church), fled to hills then Tulares. Ripoll "sat at a window at the presidio overlooking the mission, weeping."
features.result: Chumash strategic withdrawal to Tulares — retained autonomy for 4 months

sources.citation: Ripoll, Levantamiento, MS. Ord, Ocurrencias, MS., 7-11. Bancroft II, 530-531.

features.name: Recapture of La Purísima
features.summary: Lt. Estrada: 109 men (16 artillery, 23 cavalry, 35 infantry, 35 Indian auxiliaries) + 4-pounder. 8 AM attack. 400 neophytes returned fire from cannon, swivels, muskets + arrows. Neophyte guns ineffective — "ignorance of gunners." Canon may have burst, killing defenders. Cavalry cut off escape. P. Rodríguez interceded. Over at 10:30 AM. Indians: 16 killed, many wounded. Spanish: 3 wounded (1 mortal). 7 sentenced to death + shot. 4 ringleaders: 10 yrs exile.
features.result: Mexican victory — 2.5 hour battle; 7 Chumash leaders executed

sources.citation: Estrada report (Dept. St. Pap. I.166-9, C-A 27). Gonzalez, Experiencias, MS. Bancroft II, 532-534.

features.name: Estanislao's First Revolt — Sánchez Defeated
features.summary: ESTANISLAO — "a neophyte of more than ordinary ability, educated at Mission San José, of which establishment he was at one time alcalde." Ran away 1827-28 with CIPRIANO. By Nov 1828 PP. Durán and Viader warned Martínez of general rising. Earlier ~20-man expedition accomplished nothing. Estanislao continued "insulting challenges to the soldiers." May 5-8: Alf. Sánchez with ~40 men + damaged swivel-gun. Divided into 6 parties of 6 under Cpls. Piña, Berreyesa, Soto. 2 of Piña's men "so rash as to enter the wood" — killed. "Ammunition exhausted, men tired out, weather excessively hot." Estanislao left unconquered. Cpl. Soto and pvts. Peña and Pacheco praised for gallantry.
features.result: INDIGENOUS VICTORY — Sánchez's force repulsed with heavy losses (~50% casualty rate)

quote.es: Duhaut-Cilly claims Sánchez "could not get at the Indian warriors, but killed 30 women and children, and with this shameful glory returned."

sources.citation: Sánchez, Campaña contra Estanislao, 1829, MS. Durán/Viader to Martínez Nov 1828 (Arch. Arzob. V.i.68-70). Beebe & Senkewicz, 366-412. Bancroft III, 110-112.

features.name: Estanislao's Revolt — Vallejo's Expedition
features.summary: VALLEJO'S 3RD COMMAND — prior campaigns: Sierra Nevada from S. Miguel (1 killed, 15 wounded), Tulares (35 men, "slain 48 Indians, no casualties"). 107 men + 3-pounder. Set impenetrable wood on fire. Sánchez with 25 men fought 2+ hrs in burning wood. Vallejo with 37 men found "pits and ditches arranged with considerable skill" — "Indians could never have been dislodged except by the agency employed." Pursued to Arroyo Seco: "some negotiations" then Indians declared "they would die rather than surrender." Cut road through chaparral with axes; 8 wounded; ammunition ran out. Night: Indians escaped one by one. Morning: only dead bodies + 3 women. ATROCITY: Piña records 6+ captives killed. Osio: "order of no quarter given by Vallejo, infuriated auxiliaries wrought terrible carnage, pits for defences utilized as graves." Fiscal concluded: only 1 man + 1 woman killed — woman "unjustifiably by soldier Joaquín Alvarado." Martínez: "no decisive results obtained." Pardoned Oct 7, 1829.
features.result: Pyrrhic Mexican victory — Estanislao escaped; atrocity investigation

quote.en: YOSCOLO CONNECTION: Davis reports Yoscolo later seized 200 girls from Sta Clara, joined Estanislao in Mariposa. Vallejo "outgeneralled by the Indians." Yoscolo's head later displayed on pole at Sta Clara.

sources.citation: Vallejo, Campaña, 1829. Piña, Diario, 1829. Osio, Hist. Cal., 126-38. Davis, Glimpses, 282-9. Vallejo Doc. XX.280 (prior campaigns). Bancroft III, 110-114.

features.name: Vallejo vs. Guapos (Sotoyomes)
features.summary: VALLEJO'S SONOMA FRONTIER. Cainameros (allies near Sta Rosa) attacked by Sotoyomes/"Guapos" — 22 killed, 50 wounded. Chiefs appealed to Vallejo. Apr 1: 50 soldiers + 100 Indians + Cainamero force. Salvador Vallejo commanded one division. Apr 4: Battle in Geyser region hills. Guapos "routed and driven back to their rancherías, where most of them were killed." Apr 7: back at Sonoma, zero casualties. Jun 7: TREATY with 7 tribes (Yoloytoy, Guilitoy, Ansactoy, Liguaytoy, Aclutoy, Churuptoy, Guapos). Approved by Gov. Chico. 1837: Chief Zampay of Yoloytoy revolted — captured by Solano + Salvador Vallejo; eventually became "peaceful citizen and industrious farmer."
features.result: Decisive Mexican victory — zero casualties; 7-tribe treaty followed

quote.en: Bancroft: Vallejo's Indian policy "excellent and effective when compared with any other policy ever followed in California."

sources.citation: Vallejo Doc. III.104-105 (orders, report). III.119,217 (treaty). Dept. St. Pap. Ben. Mil. LXXXI.25-7. Bancroft IV, 70-72.

features.name: Battle of Soscol — Horse Thieves
features.summary: 50 Indian horse-thieves crossed Sacramento, appeared at Soscol with band of tame horses, aiming to stampede Sonoma horses. 34 killed in battle with Vallejo's men; rest surrendered. Chief CUMUCHÍ shot at Sonoma for his crimes.
features.result: Decisive Mexican victory — 34 killed, 0 Mexican casualties

features.name: Capture of Zampay (Yoloytoy)
features.summary: Chief ZAMPAY of the Yoloytoy broke the 1836 treaty, "committing many outrages and trying to arouse the Sotoyomes again." Head chief MOTI offered to aid his capture. Combined forces of SOLANO and SALVADOR VALLEJO effected capture. Zampay held captive at Sonoma but eventually "became a peaceful citizen and industrious farmer."

quote.en: Before this expedition Vallejo organized a company of 44 Suisunes and Napas, "armed and equipped like Mexican soldiers," under Lt. Sabas Fernandez, given to Solano as bodyguard.

sources.citation: Vallejo, Doc., MS., iv. 250,256. Salvador Vallejo, Notas Hist., MS., 7-65. Bancroft IV, 72.

features.name: Sacking of Pacheco/Sánchez Ranchos
features.summary: Indians sacked ranchos of Pacheco and Sánchez near San Juan Bautista: killed 1 white man, "outraging several women, burning the buildings, and destroying all they could lay their hands on." Sánchez rancho also attacked March 1837 (2 Indians killed). Riflemen at Monterey demanded $2 each before pursuing.

sources.citation: Vallejo, Doc., MS., v. 116-17, 129. Bancroft IV, 75-76.

features.name: Death of Yozcolo at Santa Clara
features.summary: Party of YOZCOLOS attacked neophytes guarding wheat-fields at Santa Clara, killing 1. Leader DIOSCULO/YOZCOLO was killed and "his head set up on a pole at the mission." This is the same Yoscolo linked to Estanislao by Davis — confirmed ex-neophyte resistance leader.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Miss., MS., ix. 60-1. Vallejo, Doc., MS., viii. 4, 41. Bancroft IV, 76.

features.name: Prado Mesa Ambush on Río de Estanislao
features.summary: Alf. PRADO MESA on expedition against thieves on "Río de Estanislao" (Stanislaus River). Surprised by the foe. 3 citizen volunteers killed, Mesa wounded, 6 of his men wounded, many weapons lost. Desiderio Briones left behind — found 7 days later by Francisco Palomares. "This disaster caused much excitement and alarm. The Indians became bolder than ever."
features.result: INDIGENOUS VICTORY — Mexican force routed with heavy losses

sources.citation: Vallejo, Doc., MS., xxv.249, viii.368-395. Bancroft IV, 76.

features.name: Sonoma Native Infantry Revolt
features.summary: Vallejo's own Indian infantry company — "about 25 selected Indians given Spanish names on the rosters, armed with muskets" — attempted revolt. "Many of the number were killed in a fight, and nine were subsequently shot." A trained indigenous military unit turned against its commanders.

sources.citation: Vallejo, Doc., MS., viii. 192; xiv. 18; xxxiii. 56-7. Bancroft IV, 74.

features.name: CLEAR LAKE MASSACRE — Salvador Vallejo
features.summary: LARGEST SINGLE-DAY DEATH TOLL IN CALIFORNIA. Capt. SALVADOR VALLEJO: 70 Californians + 200 auxiliary Indians. 12 soldiers + 30 auxiliaries reached island of "Moth" by rafts. Night attack 8-11 PM. 170 INDIANS KILLED. Official report placed island "near Cape Mendocino" — actually Clear Lake. Bojorges: real objective was slave-raiding ("to obtain Indian laborers and servants"). Dr. Sandels: attack was "in the absence of all the fighting men." Negro deserter "shot in the back." Governor ordered investigation — result unknown. Tribes: Mottiyomi, Chiliyomi, Holiyomi, Tuliyomi, Supuyomi, + 4 more.

quote.en: Precursor to 1850 U.S. Army "Bloody Island" massacre at same lake by Lt. Nathaniel Lyon.

sources.citation: Salvador Vallejo to M.G. Vallejo, Mar 13 (Vallejo Doc. XI.342). Report Apr 1 (XI.354). Governor investigation order Apr 26 (XI.366). Bojorges, Recuerdos, 24-37. Bancroft IV, 162-163.

features.name: Chaguanosos Steal 1,200 Horses from SLO
features.summary: CHAGUANOSOS (Shawnees from New Mexico, under Canadian chiefs, in league with trappers). Stole 1,200 horses from San Luis Obispo. "Well armed, ready for any kind of profitable adventure." Pursuers from LA saw "the foe retiring deliberately and independently" but "thought it imprudent to risk a conflict, especially when they saw that among the Chaguanosos there were more Americans than Indians."
features.result: Raiders escaped unchallenged — Mexicans too afraid to attack

sources.citation: Dept. St. Pap., Angeles, MS., iv.72,88. Bancroft IV, 77.

features.name: Indian Attacks near San Diego
features.summary: Late-period indigenous military action near San Diego. Juana Machado's firsthand account describes continued frontier violence in the Mexican period.

features.name: Indian Attacks near Guadalupe
features.summary: Late frontier violence near Guadalupe (Santa Barbara County). José Luciano Espinosa and María Gracia accounts describe indigenous raids in the final years before American conquest.

sources.citation: Espinosa & María Gracia accounts (Beebe & Senkewicz, pp. 420–427).

features.name: Castillo de San Joaquín fires on the Saturnina (friendly fire)
features.summary: Earliest known shore-battery firing at San Francisco. Hermenegildo Sal's guard sighted a sail at 8 a.m. and fired a warning shot; getting no reply, manned the Cantil Blanco battery (8 cannon — the future Castillo de San Joaquín / Fort Point) intending to sink her, and fired 8 cannon-shots from 11 a.m. until 5 p.m. Pilot Juan Carrasco then identified her as the Spanish navy schooner Saturnina, carrying Bodega y Quadra's Nootka dispatches, driven in for water.
features.result: Friendly-fire blunder — predates the 1803 Lelia Byrd as a battery-fires-on-ship episode

sources.citation: C-A 55 i.304–5 / scan n68–69 (Doc 135). ⚠ Check Bancroft.

features.name: English ships at Bodega Bay (Indian intelligence)
features.summary: An Indian "de las Islas" reports two ships at Puerto de la Bodega, ashore 4 days taking water and wood, hunting deer, and trading otter skins for cattle; the sailors believed English. Sal to Arrillaga. Foreign use of unguarded Bodega as the back door to SF Bay during the Nootka-crisis / Vancouver season. Related to #41 (1793 English ship at Bodega with cannons on land).
features.result: Coastal-defense intelligence — consecutive-year foreign presence at the bay Spain meant to occupy

features.name: The "Sochem" engagement
features.summary: After gentiles killed a Christian Indian of Mission San Miguel, an alférez + 5 soldiers seized 3 of the killers; on the return ~300 armed Indians attacked the patrol. The soldiers killed 1, wounded 2 and withdrew, but the alférez's horse was killed and the 3 captives escaped. Grajera then fortified against a feared assault on the mission.

sources.citation: C-A 7 Doc 567 (E29), tomo xiii.507–9. ⚠ Location inferred (San Miguel de la Frontera, SD/Baja district).

features.name: San Luis Obispo Indian conspiracy
features.summary: A thwarted mission uprising: instigators Miguel and Marito used beads as recruitment currency; La Purísima gentiles were jailed and set to forced labor; the commandant downplayed it. Part of the 1794 unrest arc with the Santa Clara / SF crisis (#43); recurs in Arrillaga's "Estado de las Californias" (ringleaders sent to SF-fort forced labor).

features.name: The Charquín uprising at Mission San Francisco
features.summary: The Christian Indian Charquín rebelled at Mission San Francisco, took up arms against the mission Christians, and held ~20 mission women and children captive in the sierra; reported by Sgt. Miguel Pacheco. An early-1790s armed mission revolt led by a baptized Indian — a precursor to the Bay Area resistance cluster (#7 / #9).

features.name: Capture & execution of the outlaw Pomponio
features.summary: From Ignacio Martínez's hoja de servicios: with only a cabo + 2 soldiers he ran down and captured the cimarrón Christian José/Pomponio at the cañada de Novato after a firefight (one comrade wounded). Guilty of "innumerables robos, homicidios" (one victim a soldier), Pomponio was shot at Monterey by government order. The death sentence survives in C-A 17 Doc 389, a Consejo de Guerra condemning him for killing soldado Manuel Varela.
features.result: Spanish counter-banditry — Pomponio executed at Monterey

sources.citation: C-A 18 LXVI.962–3 / n418–19 (Doc 365); C-A 17 Doc 389. ⚠ Date discrepancy — the standard account dates the execution Sept 1824; Savage's copy dates the sentence Feb 1821.

features.name: The Solís Revolt
features.summary: A convict-sergeant pronunciamiento: presidiario Sgt. Joaquín Solís and subcomisario José Mª Herrera seized Monterey against Gov. Echeandía; marched south; the SF garrison fired a salute and raised the flag to welcome them; defeated at Santa Bárbara (Pacheco's counter-revolution). Leaders deported to San Blas on the Volunteer. Prosecution built a "revolución por España" charge and named a foreign-merchant circle (Hartnell/Spence/Anderson/Stearns) on both sides.
features.result: Mexican-period military coup — suppressed; leaders deported

features.name: Post-Victoria Civil War (Ávila/Sánchez vs. Zamorano)
features.summary: After Gov. Victoria's overthrow, Vicente Sánchez and the convict José Antonio Ávila armed a band of presidiarios (weapons from Santa Bárbara) against Zamorano's northern faction. Ávila's party split from Sánchez past San Buenaventura and surrendered at Mission Santa Inés; dismissed under the 25 Apr 1832 amnesty. The endpoint of the Solís→anti-Victoria arc.

features.name: Estanislao-campaign atrocity inquiry
features.summary: Fiscal Ignacio Martínez investigates Indians of San José and Santa Clara killed on a punitive expedition under an alférez "de Vallejo" (⚠ likely M. G. Vallejo), including an elderly gentile woman killed by a Monterey soldier — "no le es permitido á ninguna persona atacar á los del sexo femenino." Inferred to be the aftermath of Vallejo's May–June 1829 Estanislao campaign.

features.name: Pursuit & killing of the fugitive Valerio
features.summary: Alf. Rodrigo del Pliego's hoja de servicios: caught the cimarrón Indian Valerio "herido de un balazo, de cuyas resultas murió luego," 19 Jan 1828. Almost certainly the noted Santa Bárbara-region fugitive neophyte Valerio.

features.name: Pacification of the Mission San Fernando Indians
features.summary: Del Pliego's record: with 20 men, captured the cabecillas and brought them prisoner to Santa Bárbara (21 Jul 1828).

features.name: San Diego Indian-uprising conspiracy
features.summary: A planned assault on the SD presidio — "acabar con los blancos p[ar]a ser independientes, y gobernarse ellos mismos" — extending to the missions up to San Gabriel. Ringleaders Molina and Tesochi got 2 years (attenuated for "ignorancia"); the authorities conceded "la probabilidad de los malos tratamientos" as motive. Figueroa confirmed 22 Nov 1833.

features.name: New Mexico horse-thief expedition into the Tulares
features.summary: After New Mexico "aventureros/ladrones" (allied with Tulares gentiles) drove off ~1,000 head (incl. 108 mules from Mission San Miguel; SLO stock recovered near Arroyo Grande), Gov. Figueroa ordered a 15-cavalry punitive expedition under Alf. Dámaso Rodríguez "á perseguir y destruir" the band. An early Old-Spanish-Trail horse-raiding record.

features.name: Felipe Briones killed at Cerro de Bolbones
features.summary: Felipe Briones killed trying to recover horses from Indians near the Cerro de Bolbones (Mt Diablo area).

sources.citation: Vallejo, Doc., MS., ix. 20. Bancroft IV, 76 n70.

features.name: Martínez defeats Indians near Mt Diablo
features.summary: Lt. Martínez defeated Indians near Mt Diablo, "Marsh with other Americans rendering valuable service." Report dated Feb 7.

features.name: Sutter's Indian executions
features.summary: Indians surrendered at a lake 30 mi south of the fort; "14 were put to death." Vallejo says a force of Californians sent to the valley multiple times rescued captives and took ~80 prisoners.

features.name: Cochrane squadron raid on Mission Todos Santos
features.summary: Ships of "la Escuadra del Lord Cochrane" entered Cabo de Cortés and attacked Mission Todos Santos at night; the inhabitants defended themselves, killing the lieutenant + 4 men, mortally wounding 3, and capturing the rest — a 13-man landing party. Gov. Solá ordered church silver withdrawn from the coast.

sources.citation: C-A 54 Doc 2051 (tomo II, orig. 456+; scan n211–212): Solá's circular of 30 Mar 1823 on the raid. Cochrane's squadron operated off the Californias in 1822.

features.notes: The Savage copy dates Solá’s circular 30 Mar 1823; the raid itself belongs to the 1822 Cochrane operations. Event year shown as circa 1822.

features.name: Ambush near San Vicente / Santa Catalina
features.summary: An escort transporting 5 captured Indians was ambushed: 2 soldiers killed — Juan Camacho + one other — beaten on the head with stones, their muskets found loaded but broken; the prisoners escaped; the bodies buried at San Vicente. Distinct from #37 (the 1804 Santa Catalina Spanish victory).

sources.citation: C-A 54 iv.346–9 / scan n468–470. ⚠ Pin the exact year.

features.name: Spanish Yuma Punitive Campaign
features.summary: Neve and Capt. José Antonio Romeu (108 men) march down the Colorado; Romeu with 40 men and 12 Indian allies (Jalchedunes/Cocomaricopas) routs the Yuma cavalry ('crecida mortandad'), 4 soldiers wounded; Fages escorts the rescued captives. An abortive plan to re-fortify the crossing follows. Neve's 'Antecedentes de una mortandad' names the revolt's causes: gifts withheld from Palma, women seized, mules destroying the mesquite.
features.result: Spanish reprisal after the 1781 Yuma victory

features.name: Tule balsa shipwreck at the Farallones
features.summary: Mission San Francisco Indians navigated by sea in tule balsas to evangelize gentiles on the opposite shore; currents carried one balsa onto the Farallones, where 2 of the 4 Indians drowned. Not a military engagement — an indigenous open-water navigation datum at the Bay mouth.

features.name: Horse-theft raid & punitive expedition (140-head caballada)
features.summary: 'Indios malhechores' drove off a caballada of 140 head from the national rancho; Gov. Argüello sent Alf. Santiago Argüello with 34 troops to punish the raiders and the rancherías that harbor them ('por cortar de raiz los daños'). In a related San Diego–frontier action, 14 of 15 neophyte auxiliaries who fought were killed, and ~40 raiders later burned a San Diego rancho.
features.notes: Rancherías not located in the abstract — pin placed in the Monterey-district interior, conjectural. Verbatim re-read flagged in the register.

features.name: Surrender of the Spanish squadron at Monterey
features.summary: The Spanish ship-of-the-line Asia and brig Constante — Spain's last Pacific force after the loss of Peru — put into Monterey and capitulated to the Mexican Nation, their crews swearing 'el solemne juramento de sujetarse á las leyes de los Estados Unidos Mejicanos.' Argüello tapped the Asia's powder to arm the near-empty San Francisco and Monterey batteries; pilot Juan Malarín carried the acta de Capitulación to Mexico City.

sources.citation: Dep. Rec. (C-A 46), Docs 49–52, 102; cf. Bancroft, Hist. Cal., III

features.name: The anti-Victoria revolt — the Cahuenga encounter
features.summary: California's first successful gubernatorial overthrow. The Plan of San Diego (29 Nov 1831) pronounced against Gov. Manuel Victoria 'por recobrar la representación popular'; Victoria was wounded at the Cahuenga encounter ('estado moribundo') and deported on the Pocahontas. The interregnum then splintered — the Diputación installed Pío Pico, Los Angeles proclaimed Echeandía, Zamorano rose in the north — 'casi inevitable el derramamiento de sangre.'
features.notes: Pin at Cahuenga Pass, the revolt's one armed encounter; the movement spanned San Diego to Monterey. Verbatim re-read flagged.

features.name: The anti-Micheltorena revolt — Cahuenga/Providencia
features.summary: The last Californio overthrow of a Mexican-appointed governor. The Asamblea Departamental 'desconoce la autoridad… de M. Micheltorena,' charging his 'relaciones secretas con el extranjero Sutter' (Sutter marched for Micheltorena; M.G. Vallejo corresponded against him). Micheltorena and his cholo battalion were defeated and expelled after the Battle of Cahuenga/Providencia, installing the native-born Pío Pico as California's last Mexican governor.
features.notes: The Sutter alliance prefigures the American takeover 16 months later. Verbatim re-read flagged (incl. the deposition-session date).

features.name: Ambush of the San Diego pursuit party (Dominican frontier)
features.summary: A San Diego troop detachment pursuing Dominican-mission cimarrones (runaway neophytes) was ambushed on the Baja frontier; the action left 15 Indians dead, including a leader from Mission San Miguel — a frontier engagement in the same season as the Chumash Revolt further north.
features.result: Indigenous tactical victory over a presidial party
features.notes: Ambush site not located in the abstract — pin placed on the Dominican-mission frontier, conjectural.

features.name: Gentile attack on mission neophytes — '21 neophytes killed'
features.summary: A report of 21 neophytes killed by gentiles on the interior frontier of the San Diego district — evidence for the raiding-economy pressure on mission populations in the 1820s, and for inter-indigenous violence between mission and independent communities.
features.notes: Location not given in the abstract — conjectural pin in the SD interior.

features.name: Martínez's 'campaña en los Ríos'
features.summary: Ignacio Martínez's 1827 campaign into the Delta rivers against the runaway and rebel Indians of Missions San José and Santa Clara — 2 soldiers killed. The precursor operation to the 1829 Estanislao campaigns, in the same theater.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53), Doc 8, tomo pp. 21–23

features.name: Vallejo's & Sánchez's punitive horse-raid expeditions
features.summary: Stock-raid reprisal operations by Vallejo and Sánchez in the Monterey district interior, mounted while the Monterey troops were destitute (the pay-mutiny backdrop, six months before the Solís revolt).
features.notes: Routes not located in the abstract — conjectural pin in the Monterey interior.

features.name: The great 1840 horse-raid campaign ('agua de Ramón')
features.summary: A multi-week mounted campaign, not a single skirmish. Chaguanosos drove off three mare-herds from Mission San Gabriel (14 May); the LA vecino-militia mobilized (13→36 men, 4 muskets, 19 lances); Ignacio Palomares's ~23-man party was routed; relief columns held the Puerta del Cajón and recovered the herds at the 'agua de Ramón' surprise. Leandri's report: the rearguard was '20 hombres Ciudadanos de los Estados Unidos; pero tenían á más otra gente,' driving 1,000+ head, ~1,500 driven to death in flight.
features.result: Raiders routed one militia party; herds recovered at agua de Ramón
features.notes: Pin at Cajon Pass, the campaign's hinge; 'agua de Ramón' itself is unlocated (secondary lit puts the fight at Resting Springs — the partes' 'Ramón' is a datum to weigh vs Bancroft IV:77, not to harmonize).

features.name: Plaza firefight (Alvarado civil war)
features.summary: Californio-vs-Californio action of the 1838 civil war (the Alvarado–Carrillo contest for the governorship): per José Castro's abstracted report, a 160-man force garrisoning 'la plaza' abandoned it by night after a 2-day continuous firefight with only 1 dead, pursued by cavalry; two bronze cannon at the Castillo were transferred to José Estrada.
features.result: Garrison abandoned 'la plaza' after a 2-day firefight

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., Monterey (C-A 43), Doc 4 (n420, tomo 41)

features.notes: 'La plaza' is not named on the leaf — probably Santa Bárbara or a southern plaza. Conjectural pin; transcribe the parte before locating or quoting.

features.name: Ibarra's punitive battle at Santa Isabel ('veinte pares de orejas')
features.summary: A punitive action against ~100 or more hostile Indians, routed 4 leguas to San Felipe with Kumeyaay/Cahuilla auxiliaries; 28 killed, '20 pares de orejas' cut from the dead as proof, plus a prisoner summarily executed.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. Ben., Pref. y Juzg. (C-A 42), Doc 914 (Expanded Entry 26)

features.notes: Abstract — transcribe the parte before quoting the orejas line.

features.name: José Castro's 120-man northern expedition 'contra los bárbaros'
features.summary: A late-Mexican state campaign against interior raiders — 120 men under Castro 'contra los bárbaros que invaden los puntos del norte.'
features.result: State campaign (outcome not recorded in the abstract)

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. Ben., Pref. y Juzg. (C-A 42), Doc 1228

features.notes: 'Los puntos del norte' unlocated — conjectural pin on the northern frontier.

features.name: The Gavilán (Hawk's Peak) standoff
features.summary: Frémont fortifies Gavilán Peak and raises the US flag; Castro musters the militia at San Juan; Frémont withdraws north by night without an engagement — the armed face-off that preceded the conquest by three months, with the prefecture's intelligence file (the Guerrero/neophyte-Antonio reports on the '200 armed foreigners').
features.result: Standoff — Frémont withdrew without an engagement

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. Ben., Pref. y Juzg. (C-A 42), Docs 361–363 (Expanded Entry 22)

features.name: The Rancho San Francisco killing of 18 'indios ladrones'
features.summary: Twenty-one armed Indians were disarmed 'mañosamente,' revolted with stones and knives, and were shot — 18 dead, 2 wounded, 1 escaped: the anti-raiding apparatus at its most lethal, weeks before the conquest.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. Ben., Pref. y Juzg. (C-A 42), Doc 1219 (n412)

features.notes: District-court abstract — verify vs leaf before quoting.

features.name: San Pedro Mártir Indian revolt (Dominican frontier)
features.summary: Mission Indians revolt ('el alboroto'); the officer blamed for causing it is imprisoned at San Vicente on Borica's approval; rebels are seized and prosecuted via sumaria (Bernal spends 24 days at the mission); the investigation runs into 1797 (declarations of the indio José Manuel, later freed); the mission's interpreter is killed by mid-1797.
features.result: Revolt suppressed judicially; the blamed officer imprisoned
features.notes: A revolt + judicial-military response arc, not a field battle.

features.name: Río Colorado engagement ('atacar la fuerza con la fuerza')
features.summary: The commander, met with insults from 'una colonia numerosa' of Indians on all sides in bad terrain, 'se vió precisado á atacar la fuerza con la fuerza.' Losses: Spanish, a mule and 7–8 horses; Indian, 7 gandules killed. Reported to the Viceroy as proof the Sonora communication could not be secured without real garrisons; the Viceroy later reviewed a printed account of the engagement.
features.result: Spanish column fought through — 7 Indians killed

sources.citation: Prov. Rec. (C-A 24), Doc 313 (n270, Sav 271, tomo p. 697)

features.notes: Site unlocated — conjectural pin near the lower Colorado. Abstract — transcribe before quoting.

features.name: Punitive battle near San Juan Bautista — death of captain 'Talholostl'[?]
features.summary: Gentiles 'de la otra banda' killed 5 Indians and 2 women of the mission rancherías; Borica sent Sgt. Macario Castro with 10 cuera soldiers, 8 Christian Indians, and 24 aggrieved gentiles; the column caught the malefactors, killed the enemy captain 'Talholostl'[?], recovered 2 women, and captured 2 — a mixed Spanish–Native punitive force whose gentile allies were the majority.
features.result: Punitive column killed the enemy captain

sources.citation: Prov. Rec. (C-A 24), Sav 430 (probe n431) — Borica → Viceroy, 'Pelea con Indios'

features.notes: The indigenous name is uncertain on the leaf — verbatim transcription needed.

features.name: The Jacum battle — capitanejo Charagui defeats the San Diego column
features.summary: Twenty-five soldados and 50 gentiles auxiliares struck Jacum; 'el capitanejo Charagui… cargó con toda su gente y rodeando nuestras tropas nos las hicieron retirar, hiriéndonos á 9 soldados y 17 indios de los auxiliares… quedó el campo pr los enemigos.' The raiders had already run off the Misión San Diego caballada. Embedded in Zamorano's extermination-rhetoric letter; aftermath, Alvarado ordered all aid to Capt. Portilla for the pursuit (Aug 1837).
features.result: Indigenous victory — the field held by Charagui's force

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., Angeles (C-A 37), Doc 188 (n87–n90, Sav 86–89, orig. pp. 420–424); aftermath Doc 193

features.notes: Charagui's people are named only as gentiles of the Jacum region in the abstract.

features.name: Mission San Diego de Alcalá
features.summary: 1769. First mission.

features.name: Presidio of San Diego
features.summary: 1769. Punta de Guijarros battery.

features.name: Mission Santa Bárbara
features.summary: 1786. Presidio 1782.

features.name: Presidio of Monterey
features.summary: 1770. Capital.

features.name: Mission San Rafael
features.summary: 1817. Site selected by Moraga.

features.name: Fort Ross (Russian)
features.summary: 1812. Russian-American Company.
